{{short description|Social Psychologist}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=March 2020}}

'''Heejung Kim''' is a South Korean psychologist and a professor in the Department of Psychological & Brain Sciences at the [[University of California, Santa Barbara]].<ref name="KimFacultyProfile">{{Cite web|url=https://www.psych.ucsb.edu/people/faculty/heejung-kim|title=Heejung Kim {{!}} Psychological & Brain Sciences {{!}} UCSB|website=www.psych.ucsb.edu|access-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103042104/https://www.psych.ucsb.edu/people/faculty/heejung-kim|archive-date=3 November 2019|url-status=live}}</ref> Her research focuses on how culture influences humans' thought process.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=http://spsp.org/news-center/announcements/welcome-to-new-senior-editorial-team|title=New PSPR Editors|last=|first=|date=5 October 2017|website=Society for Personality and Social Psychology|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103042112/http://spsp.org/news-center/announcements/welcome-to-new-senior-editorial-team|archive-date=3 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=http://heejung.kim.socialpsychology.org/|title=Heejung Kim|last=|first=|date=|website=Social Psychology Network|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103042105/http://heejung.kim.socialpsychology.org/|archive-date=3 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> She is co-editor of the journal ''[[Personality and Social Psychology Review]]''.<ref name="SageJournal">{{Cite web|url=https://us.sagepub.com/en-us/nam/personality-and-social-psychology-review/journal201783|title=Personality and Social Psychology Review|last=|first=|date=28 October 2015|website=[[SAGE Publications Inc]]|language=en-us|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190523183531/https://us.sagepub.com/en-us/nam/personality-and-social-psychology-review/journal201783|archive-date=23 May 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref name=":0" />

== Career ==
Kim received her first Bachelors of Arts degree in French Literature from [[Ewha Womans University]] in Seoul, Korea.<ref name=KimBrief2010>Kim, H. (June 2010). [https://www.apa.org/science/about/psa/2010/06/sci-brief Culture and self-expression] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190523183519/https://www.apa.org/science/about/psa/2010/06/sci-brief |date=23 May 2019 }}. ''Psychological Science Agenda''</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=https://taylorlab.psych.ucla.edu/members/|title=Members {{!}} UCLA Social Neuroscience Lab|last=|first=|date=|website=UCLA Social Neuroscience Lab|language=en-US|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190622133942/https://taylorlab.psych.ucla.edu/members/|archive-date=22 June 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> She earned a second Bachelor of Arts degree in Psychology from the [[University of Southern California]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=KimBrief2010/> She completed her Masters of Arts and PhD program in Social Psychology at [[Stanford University]] in 2001.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|url=http://spsp.org/sites/default/files/dialogue222.pdf|title=Citation Counts, Prestige Measurement, and Graduate Training in Social Psychology|last=Phillips|first=Nia|date=Fall 2007|journal=Dialogue: The Official Newsletter of the Society for Personality and Social Psychology|access-date=3 November 2019|issue=2|volume=22|pages=24–27}}</ref><ref name="KimFacultyProfile" /> In 2002, she began working at [[Harvey Mudd College]] as an assistant professor of Humanities and Social Sciences.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.science.org/content/article/changing-guard-harvey-mudd|title=The Changing of the Guard at Harvey Mudd|last1=Ringen|first1=Randy|last2=Austin|first2=Jim|date=26 October 2001|website=Science Magazine|language=en|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103044133/https://www.sciencemag.org/careers/2001/10/changing-guard-harvey-mudd|archive-date=3 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> In 2003, she accepted the position of assistant professor at the [[University of California, Santa Barbara]] (UCSB), where she currently works as a professor in the Department of Psychological and Brain Sciences.<ref name="KimFacultyProfile" /><ref name=":2" /> Kim directs the Cultural Psychology Lab at UCSB where she investigates how one's culture can shape psychological processes, decision making, religion, biology, and interpersonal communication.<ref name="KimLab">{{Cite web|url=https://labs.psych.ucsb.edu/kim/heejung/|title=Welcome to the UCSB Cultural Psychology Laboratory|last=|first=|date=|website=UCSB Cultural Psychology Lab|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190523183529/https://labs.psych.ucsb.edu/kim/heejung/|archive-date=23 May 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> She is a collaborator for the [[UCLA]] Social Neuroscience Lab.<ref name=":2" />

Kim has worked for several publications. She worked as an associate editor for the ''[[Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin]]'' (''PSPB''),<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cornelissen|first1=Gert|last2=Dewitte|first2=Siegfried|last3=Warlop|first3=Luk|date=25 April 2011|title=Are Social Value Orientations Expressed Automatically? Decision Making in the Dictator Game|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|language=en|volume=37|issue=8|pages=1080–1090|doi=10.1177/0146167211405996|pmid=21518808|s2cid=14268850 }}</ref><ref name=":0" /> and was on the editorial board of ''[[Psychological Science]]'' in 2011.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.psychologicalscience.org/publications/psychological_science/2011-editorial-board|title=Psychological Science Editorial Board|last=|first=|date=|website=Association for Psychological Science|language=en-US|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103050636/https://www.psychologicalscience.org/publications/psychological_science/2011-editorial-board|archive-date=3 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> From 2012 to 2014, she was the associate editor at the ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'' (''JPSP'').<ref>Editorial Board list retrieved from ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', 102(1) on November 5, 2019.</ref><ref>Editorial Board list retrieved from ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', 104(1) on November 5, 2019.</ref><ref>Editorial Board list retrieved from ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', 106(1) on November 5, 2019.</ref> Kim is currently a consulting editor for the Interpersonal Relations and Group Processes Section in JPSP. From 2018 to 2021, Kim will be holding the role of co-editor at ''[[Personality and Social Psychology Review]]'' with her UCSB colleague, professor David Sherman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Editorial Board: Interpersonal Relations and Group Processes Section|url=https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/psp/edboard-irgp|website=[[American Psychological Association]]|access-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103051516/https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/psp/edboard-irgp|archive-date=3 November 2019|url-status=live}}</ref>

Kim has held multiple positions at the [[Society for Personality and Social Psychology]] such as being a symposium reviewer for the Convention: Program Committee in 2011 and an adjunct member of the fellows committee in 2015.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|url=https://my.spsp.org/members/leadership|title=Leadership Directory|last=|first=|date=|website=Society of Experimental Social Psychology|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191108230806/https://my.spsp.org/members/leadership|archive-date=8 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> Since 2016, Kim has been a member of the Summer Institute for Social Psychology and Personality and was recently promoted to co-chair of this committee in 2019.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://spsp.org/sites/default/files/2016%20Winter%20-%20San%20Diego.pdf|title=SPSP Board of Directors Meeting Minutes|website=[[Society of Experimental Social Psychology]]|access-date=3 November 2019|date=31 January 2016}}</ref><ref name=":3" /> She was also a mentor for the 2016 Summer Program for Undergraduate Research.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://spsp.org/news-center/announcements/spur-participants-and-mentors|title=SPUR Participants and Mentors Describe Their Experiences|last=|first=|date=|website=[[Society of Experimental Social Psychology]]|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103053509/http://spsp.org/news-center/announcements/spur-participants-and-mentors|archive-date=3 November 2019|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref>

== Research ==
Kim has authored and co-authored more than 90 scientific publications which appeared in multiple renowned journals such as the ''JPSP'', ''[[Psychological Science]]'', ''PSPB'', and ''[[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America|Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences]]''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=ItXSD98AAAAJ&hl=en|title=Heejung Kim - Google Scholar Citations|website=scholar.google.com|access-date=5 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191211153054/https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=ItXSD98AAAAJ&hl=en|archive-date=11 December 2019|url-status=live}}</ref>

Her research mostly focuses on a cultural explanation for human behaviors. One of her studies explores how cultural preferences toward uniqueness and conformity differ between East Asians and Americans.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kim|first1=Heejung|last2=Markus|first2=Hazel Rose|date=1999|title=Deviance or uniqueness, harmony or conformity? A cultural analysis.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=77|issue=4|pages=785–800|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.77.4.785|issn=0022-3514|url=http://www.escholarship.org/uc/item/81h7x2x7|access-date=20 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190403142351/https://escholarship.org/uc/item/81h7x2x7|archive-date=3 April 2019|url-status=live}}</ref> Another study discusses the cultural differences between Asians and Asian Americans group and European Americans group in how each sought and used social support.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kim|first1=Heejung S.|last2=Sherman|first2=David K.|last3=Taylor|first3=Shelley E.|date=2008|title=Culture and social support.|journal=American Psychologist|volume=63|issue=6|pages=518–526|doi=10.1037/0003-066x|pmid=18793039|s2cid=5181497 |issn=1935-990X|url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/7z9513d5|access-date=20 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190403142353/https://escholarship.org/uc/item/7z9513d5|archive-date=3 April 2019|url-status=live}}</ref> Her most recent publication focuses on a sociocultural approach to understand the motivations behind pro-environmental actions.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Eom|first1=Kimin|last2=Papadakis|first2=Viki|last3=Sherman|first3=David K.|last4=Kim|first4=Heejung S.|date=2019|title=The Psychology of Proenvironmental Support: In Search of Global Solutions for a Global Problem|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|language=en|volume=28|issue=5|pages=490–495|doi=10.1177/0963721419854099|s2cid=198637582 |issn=0963-7214|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Research in news ===
Kim's research has been quoted in multiple mainstream and scientific outlets, including ''[[Psychology Today]]'',<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/sex-murder-and-the-meaning-life/201801/does-thinking-out-loud-help-your-performance-or-hinder?collection=1111264|title=Does thinking out loud help your performance, or hinder it?|last=Kenrick|first=Douglas|date=26 January 2018|work=Psychology Today|access-date=19 November 2019}}</ref> [[Yahoo! News|Yahoo News]],<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://uk.news.yahoo.com/parents-file-lawsuit-against-school-where-teachers-colored-in-13-yearolds-haircut-with-sharpie-he-felt-extremely-degraded-200533183.html?|title=Parents file lawsuit against school where teachers colored 13-year-old's haircut with Sharpie: "He felt extremely degraded"|last=Newton|first=Kamilah|date=21 August 2019|work=Yahoo News|access-date=19 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191116222256/https://uk.news.yahoo.com/parents-file-lawsuit-against-school-where-teachers-colored-in-13-yearolds-haircut-with-sharpie-he-felt-extremely-degraded-200533183.html|archive-date=16 November 2019|url-status=live}}</ref> ''[[Wired (magazine)|Wired Magazine]]'',<ref>{{Cite magazine|url=https://www.wired.com/2010/09/the-depression-map-genes-culture-serotonin-and-a-side-of-pathogens/|title=The depression map: genes, culture, serotonin, and a side of pathogens|last=Dobbs|first=David|date=14 September 2010|magazine=Wired|access-date=5 November 2019|issn=1059-1028|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180430210316/https://www.wired.com/2010/09/the-depression-map-genes-culture-serotonin-and-a-side-of-pathogens/|archive-date=30 April 2018|url-status=live}}</ref> and [[Psych Central]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://psychcentral.com/news/2011/11/09/cultural-differences-impact-gene-environment-interactions/31234.html|title=Cultural differences impact gene-environment interactions|last=Nauert|first=Rick|date=8 August 2018|work=Psych Central|access-date=19 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111110140349/http://psychcentral.com/news/2011/11/09/cultural-differences-impact-gene-environment-interactions/31234.html|archive-date=10 November 2011|url-status=live}}</ref>

Her work on the [[oxytocin receptor]] gene OXTR has been covered by [[Ed Yong]] in [[Discover (magazine)|''Discover'' magazine]].<ref name=XaXsM>{{Cite news|url=http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2010/08/16/genes-and-culture-oxtr-gene-influences-social-behaviour-differently-in-americans-and-koreans/#.XaXsM-hKg2w|title=Genes and culture: OXTR gene influences social behaviour differently in Americans and Koreans|last=Yong|first=Ed|date=16 August 2010|work=[[Discover (magazine)|Discover]]|access-date=5 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191020060704/http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2010/08/16/genes-and-culture-oxtr-gene-influences-social-behaviour-differently-in-americans-and-koreans/#.XaXsM-hKg2w|archive-date=20 October 2019|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2010/11/29/the-dark-side-of-oxytocin-much-more-than-just-a-love-hormone/#.XZLAXUZKg2w|title=The dark side of oxytocin, much more than just a "love hormone"|last=Yong|first=Ed|date=29 November 2010|work=[[Discover (magazine)|Discover]]|access-date=5 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190902094455/http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/notrocketscience/2010/11/29/the-dark-side-of-oxytocin-much-more-than-just-a-love-hormone/#.XZLAXUZKg2w|archive-date=2 September 2019|url-status=live}}</ref> Previous research had shown that people with certain versions of the OXTR gene are more social and more likely to seek social support during times of stress; however, Kim's work suggests that this effect may be culturally specific.<ref name=XaXsM/> In her work, Americans showed this pattern, but in Koreans the "social" version of the OXTR gene was not associated with social support seeking (and the pattern suggested ''less'' social support seeking in distressed Koreans with this version of OXTR). Koreans tend to have concerns about overburdening friends and family with their problems and thus are less likely to seek social support during stress, whereas Americans are more likely to seek social support. Kim suggests that instead this version of OXTR may be related to adherence to social norms.<ref name=XaXsM/>

== Grants and awards ==
The [[National Science Foundation]] has granted Kim and her co-researchers over a million dollars in research funding. Funded projects include: Sociocultural Determinants of Human Motives, Oxytocin and Socio-Emotional Sensitivity: Mechanisms of Gene-Culture Interaction, RAPID: The Psychology of Fear: Cultural Orientation and Response to Ebola Threat, Culture, Social Support, and Managing Stress, and DHB: Collaborative Research: Cultural and Genetic Basis of Social Support Use.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=1823824|title=NSF Award Search: Award#1823824 - Sociocultural Determinants of Human Motives|website=www.nsf.gov|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=1124552|title=NSF Award Search: Award#1124552 - Oxytocin and Socio-Emotional Sensitivity: Mechanisms of Gene-Culture Interaction|website=www.nsf.gov|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=1516476|title=NSF Award Search: Award#1516476 - RAPID: The Psychology of Fear: Cultural Orientation and Response to Ebola Threat|website=www.nsf.gov|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=0338631&HistoricalAwards=false|title=NSF Award Search: Award#0338631 - Culture, Social Support, and Managing Stress|website=www.nsf.gov|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=0729532&HistoricalAwards=false|title=NSF Award Search: Award#0729532 - DHB:Collaborative Research: Cultural and Genetic Basis of Social Support Use|website=www.nsf.gov|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref>

Kim is the recipient of the 2002 Dissertation Award given by the [[Society of Experimental Social Psychology]] (SESP).<ref name=":4">{{Cite web|url=https://www.sesp.org/content.asp?contentid=148|title=Dissertation Award Recipients|last=|first=|date=|website=Society of Experimental Social Psychology|access-date=3 November 2019}}</ref> In 2013, her [[University of California, Santa Barbara]] advisee, Joni Sasaki, also won the SESP Dissertation Award, making it the second time in 41 years that both mentor and advisee were awarded this prestigious title.<ref name=":4" /><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://graduatepost.squarespace.com/headlines/tag/historical?currentPage=18|title=The GradPost at UC Santa Barbara - Gradpost Headlines|website=graduatepost.squarespace.com|language=en|access-date=3 November 2019}}{{Dead link|date=October 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}</ref> Kim was described as one of the Revolutionary Minds in science by [[Seed (magazine)|''Seed'' Magazine]] (August 2008) and was a top-cited assistant professor in ''Social Psychology'' (Dialogue, Fall, 2007).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.seedmagazine.com/archives/|title=Archives § SEEDMAGAZINE.COM|website=www.seedmagazine.com|access-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191028230450/https://www.seedmagazine.com/archives/|archive-date=28 October 2019|url-status=unfit}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artsjournal.com/flyover/2008/08/revolutionary_minds.html|title=Revolutionary Minds - flyover|website=www.artsjournal.com|access-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002170557/http://www.artsjournal.com/flyover/2008/08/revolutionary_minds.html|archive-date=2 October 2015|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":5" /> Kim became an SPSP fellow in 2012.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://spsp.org/membership/fellows|title=Fellowship {{!}} SPSP|website=spsp.org|access-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190725215147/http://spsp.org/membership/fellows|archive-date=25 July 2019|url-status=live}}</ref>
